turns.content: I've been using the standard LLVM backend for my release builds, but I noticed in a blog post that rustc_codegen_cranelift offers better performance for debug symbols. I tried enabling it via config.toml and got some output, but I'm wondering if there's a better way to configure this or if I should stick with LLVM entirely.

Enabling rustc_codegen_cranelift is definitely an option for faster symbol generation, but you need to be aware that it doesn't support all features that LLVM does. For example, certain intrinsics and optimization passes available in the LLVM backend are missing here. You might want to check if your specific use case requires those missing capabilities before switching.

That makes sense regarding the feature gaps. I'm mostly interested in getting debug info quickly without running into issues with complex Rust features like fat pointers or custom ABI rules. Is there a way to enable just the fast path while keeping full compatibility?

Currently, rustc_codegen_cranelift is a monolithic backend choice in your Cargo.toml dependencies; you cannot selectively enable only certain optimization paths or disable specific unsupported features. It either replaces the LLVM backend entirely or it doesn't work at all for your target triple.

Okay, so I can't do a hybrid approach. I was hoping to avoid breaking my existing codebase while still getting the speedup. Since rustc_codegen_cranelift is built on Cranelift, does it handle all standard Rust traits like the Write trait correctly?

Cranelift handles most standard types and traits fine, including Write, but there are edge cases where custom layouts or advanced memory safety features might not be fully supported. You should test your specific code with rustc_codegen_cranelift to ensure no unexpected behavior occurs before committing to it.

I see, so I need to validate my custom traits manually. One thing that confused me was the error messages when I tried to compile with cranelift; they seemed to reference LLVM internals even though I'm not using LLVM directly.

Those error messages are misleading because Cranelift has historically borrowed some terminology from LLVM. The actual implementation is entirely separate, but the compiler tooling layers still use similar naming conventions. You'll find that some internal logs reference LLVM components even when cranelift is the active backend.

That explains it. I think I understand now. So for a tooling project where speed matters more than absolute feature completeness, rustc_codegen_cranelift seems like a viable option if I can accept some limitations?

Exactly. If your tooling doesn't rely on those specific missing features and you prioritize compile-time speed, then enabling rustc_codegen_cranelift via config.toml is the right move. Just remember to keep an eye on unsupported features as you add new functionality to your project.
